lessons.name: The ADO framework — what to actually SAY in the room
Drill: demo fails during executive presentation
Drill: client demands 6 weeks of work in 3 days
Drill: explain stochastic model variability to a CFO
Drill: client IT blocks the integration
Drill: same problem recurs 2 hours after fix
Scoping a project with a non-technical buyer
Written status cadence — the no-meeting Slack update
When (and how) to escalate to your manager
Killing a project gracefully — when the right answer is "we shouldn't build this"

cards.q: ADO framework for handling an angry-customer / outage moment?
cards.a: Acknowledge the impact in their words, Diagnose visibly (think out loud so they see progress), Own the resolution timeline with a concrete next checkpoint. Never blame the customer's environment in the moment.

cards.q: ADO -- what concrete sentence fits each phase?
cards.a: Acknowledge: 'I hear that this is blocking your launch.' Diagnose: 'Checking the logs now -- can you confirm when it last worked?' Offer: 'I'll have a fix or a workaround by 4pm, and I'll send a written update either way.'
Acknowledge + run two tracks: visible (show recorded version) + backend (debug). Never blame customer's env in the moment. RCA within 24h.

cards.q: Customer demands 6 weeks in 3 days — move?
cards.a: Decompose to the 20% that unblocks them + document tradeoffs + get the REAL deadline (often softer than stated).

cards.q: Same problem recurs 2 hours after fix — response?
cards.a: Own as Sev1 + commit specific timeline + status updates every 4 hours. Defending the prior fix kills trust further.
Four labeled bullets: Shipped this week, In flight, Risks/blockers, Next week. 5 short bullets read better than 3 paragraphs; lead with outcomes, not activity.

cards.q: Customer's IT team blocks integration — move?
cards.a: Structured security review proposal (SOC2 docs + architecture + sub-processor list). Bypassing them = political damage.

cards.q: A customer-side champion is leaving — what next?
cards.a: Get reintroduced to replacement + re-confirm scope/timeline with new owner. Champion loss = retention risk.

cards.q: Explaining stochastic AI to a CFO?
cards.a: Weather-forecast analogy + show confidence range + 95th-percentile worst case + leading indicators. Quant always wins with CFOs.

cards.q: What does each letter of ADO actually mean?
cards.a: Acknowledge the impact in the customer's words (not 'sorry for the inconvenience'). Diagnose visibly so they see progress and can supply context. Offer a concrete next step with a named owner and a time, even if that step is just 'I'll write back by 5pm.'

cards.q: The 'I don't know' recovery pattern in a roleplay?
cards.a: Say 'I don't know yet' (not 'I don't know'), state what you would check to find out, and commit to a time you'll come back with the answer. Bluffing is the single fastest way to fail the round.

cards.q: When to push back on a customer ask vs acquiesce?
cards.a: Push back when the ask hurts the customer's own outcome (bad data, unsafe rollout, wrong metric). Acquiesce on preference items (UI choices, report format) where the cost is low. Always name the tradeoff explicitly so the choice is theirs, not yours.

cards.q: How to surface bad news to a customer?
cards.a: Lead with the headline and the impact in the first sentence, not the backstory. Then state what you're doing about it and the next checkpoint. Burying bad news mid-paragraph or waiting for the next standing meeting is the anti-pattern interviewers flag.

cards.q: Top client-sim anti-patterns that fail candidates?
cards.a: Defensiveness ('well, actually...'), blaming the customer's environment or team in the moment, over-promising a fix time you can't hit, and ending the turn without a committed next step. Any one of these is usually a no-hire signal.

cards.q: When should an FDE escalate to their manager during an engagement?
cards.a: Escalate when the issue threatens the contract, requires a resource or policy decision above your level, or when you've missed a committed timeline twice. Escalate early with a recommendation, not a problem dump -- 'here's what's happening and what I'd like to do.'

cards.q: How to negotiate scope mid-engagement without losing trust?
cards.a: Re-anchor on the original business outcome, show the current burn-down, and present two options with explicit tradeoffs (cut scope X to hit date, or slip date to keep scope X). Let the customer pick. Never silently de-scope.

cards.q: How to recommend killing a project gracefully?
cards.a: Frame it as protecting the customer's outcome, not retreating: show the data on why the current path won't hit the business goal, propose the smaller bet that would, and offer to keep the relationship warm. Credibility goes up, not down, when you say 'we shouldn't build this.'

cards.q: What signals do client-sim interviewers actually grade on?
cards.a: Composure under pressure (no flinching, no filler), customer-first framing (their outcome before your product), and the ability to commit to a concrete next step with an owner and a time. Polish matters less than ending the turn with a clear ADO.
